question: Were all restrictions in eligibility crieria based on study characteristics appropriate (e.g. date, sample size, study quality, outcomes measured)? If yes, indicate which study characteristic was an inclusion/exclusion criteria

Did the search include an appropriate range of databases/ electronic sources for published and unpublished reports?
answers: The response to treatment in our patients was compared to reports from the existing literature retrieved from PubMed, Web of Science, EMBASE, and ClinicalTrials.gov

question: Were methods additional to database searching used to identify relevant reports?
answers: Cross-references were retrieved, but this did not identify additional studies.

question: Were the terms and structure of the search strategy likely to retrieve as many eligible studies as possible?
Were search strategy restrictions based on date, publication format, or language appropriate?
answers: Studies published by August 2015 were included.

question: Were efforts made to minimise error in selection of studies?
Were efforts made to minimise error in data collection?
Were sufficient study characteristics considered for both review authors and readers to be able to interpret the results?
Were all relevant study results collected for use in the synthesis?
Was risk of bias (or methodological quality) formally assessed using appropriate criteria?
Were efforts made to minimise error in risk of bias assessment?
Did the synthesis include all studies that it should?
answers:
Our literature search identified 26 studies (five case stud-ies, 18 case series, one prospective single-center investiga-tion,  and  two  randomized,  double-blinded,  placebo-controlled pilot studies) comprising 185 (results reportedon 174) patients, with a median of four, ranging from 1to 21 patients exploring omalizumab treatment for recal-citrant AD. A total of 129 (74.1%) patients experienced a beneficial effect of treatment ranging from little to com-plete  response  (Table 2).8,10,12-28Forty-five  patients(25.9%) from seven different studies reported no or nega-tive effect off omalizumab treatment.9,29-32Ongoing tri-als (ClinicalTrials.gov) exploring the use of omalizumabin the treatment of AD are being conducted.33We identi-fied seven trials with two currently posting results (notincluded)

Our results agree with Amrol,21reporting variation inthe clinical improvement between patients. This differencein effect may reflect the clinical heterogeneity of AD.Notably the best effect was seen among the youngestpatients, suggesting a contribution of the treatment to thenatural outgrowing of AD.21As seen in the case serieswith the lowest mean age8,19,21,26(<20 years), whichreports good or excellent clinical effect on all but onepatient, who still showed mild improvement. The inter-pretation of improvement as an effect of treatment shouldaccommodate the fact that most patients with AD (75%)have a spontaneous remission of disease before adoles-cence.4It is therefore impossible to determine whether he effects reported are a combination of the two, orsolely due to the natural history of AD

question: Were the findings robust, e.g. as demonstrated through funnel plot or sensitivity analyses?
Were biases in primary studies minimal or addressed in the synthesis?

answers:
Furthermore, we systematically review the existing literature identifying studies of AD treated with omalizumab

Table 2 Reviewed cases

In the reports reviewed, eight different baseline treat-ment regimens were identified, with the initial dosageranging  from  150 mg/2 weeks  (Quistet al.29every4 weeks)8,13,14,18,23,29-32to 600 mg/2 weeks,15and withdifferent strategies for increasing dosage throughout treat-ment8,14,29,30(see comments in Table 2). The averageduration of treatment was 8.1 months, ranging from 3 to29.2 months, with two studies far exceeding averagetreatment duration.25,26The regimen 150 mg/2 weeks (or300 mg/4 weeks) was used in eight studies and herebythe most widely used. One study increased dosage untilan improvement in dermatological status was seen, with amaximum of 450 mg/2 weeks.23Yet another followedthe guidelines for treatment of asthma and dosed afterbodyweight and IgE level (0.016 mg/kg/IU).17Some stud-ies treated with lower than recommended dose andreported clinical benefit from treatment.8,31Of the fivestudies reporting negative or uncertain positive effects,four were of minimal starting dose,29-32while Krathenand Hsu9were at 450 mg/2 weeks

question: If they performed quantitative synthesis did the review authors carry out an adequate investigation of publication bias (small study bias) and discuss its likely impact on the results of the review?
